{{Chess Opening Theory/Position |Accelerated London |parent=[[../|Closed game]] |responses=
*[[/2...Nf6|2...Nf6]] *[[/2...c5|2...c5]] }} == 2. Bf4 · Accelerated London system== White develops the dark-square bishop. Developing the bishop first allows White to support their d4-pawn with e3, without trapping this bishop behind the pawn chain. {{Chess/board|fen=8/8/8/8/3P1B2/2PBPN2/PP1N1PPP/R2QK2R|caption=Illustration of White's typical set-up in the London system. White usually plays '''e3''' and '''c3''' to support d4, '''Bd3''' and '''Nd2''' to control e4, and '''Nf3''' aiming for Ne5.|frame=1|float=left}} This is the beginning of the London system. The appeal of this system is that White can go for the same general set-up in response to most of Black's replies (see diagram). General plans for White include planting their kingside knight on e5, controlled by d4 and the dark-square bishop. Often White's light-square bishop is sacrificed on h7 to open up a castled king, followed by Qh5+. Formally, the Accelerated London follows 1...d5, but the London set up can be played [[../../1...Nf6/2. Bf4|against 1...Nf6]] just as well and may transpose if Black plays d5. At higher levels, the move order 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Bf4 to reach the London system is more common. The main alternative for White is the Rapport-Jobava London system, with Nc3 (in either order, [[../2. Nc3|2. Nc3]] & 3. Bf4 or 2. Bf4 & 3. Nc3) where plans include the knight coordinating with the f4 bishop to attack c7, and an early e4. === Responses === Because White can play the same system in response to most of Black's replies, White usually continues 3. e3, 4. c3, 5. Nd2 etc regardless. Most move orders are interchangeable. Black's most common reply is [[/2...Nf6|'''2...Nf6''']], then they have two main approaches: 3...c5 or 3...e6. The idea behind the 3...e6 line is to play 4...Bd6, getting ready to castle quickly and threatening to trade off White's London bishop. White can drop their bishop back to g3, inviting Black to trade but after hxg3 White gets the semi-open h-file in return. In the e6 lines, Black's light square bishop often has difficulty developing. This approach resembles a '''Semi-Slav defence with colours reversed'''. The ...c5 line (either 3...c5, or in the order [[/2...c5|'''2...c5''']] 3. e6 Nf6) is called the '''Steinitz countergambit'''. Black's idea is to prioritise ...Bf5 and ...Qb6 before they finish developing the kingside and castling. Black seeks counterplay by pressuring b2, which White left undefended when they moved their bishop early on. (White does not want to take the offered c5 pawn of course: they'd rather play c3 and let Black trade into them, so that they can keep two pawns in the centre). Having committed to d5 already, the '''King's Indian-style''' approach with 3...g6 is less common but still playable. Black continues 4...Bg7, and 5...O-O. White usually includes h3, so there is a retreat square for their bishop on h2, and Be2.
{{Chess/board|moves=1. d4 d5 2. Bf4 Nf6 3. e3 e6 4. Nd2 Bd6 5. Bg3 O-O 6. Bd3 c5 7. c3 Qc7|caption=The '''...e6''', Reverse Semi-Slav approach: mainline position after 2...Nf6 3. e3 e6 4. Nd2 Bd6 5. Bg3 O-O 6. Bd3 c5 7. c3 Qc7.}} {{Chess/board|moves=1. d4 d5 2. Bf4 c5 3. e3 Nc6 4. c3 Nf6 5. Nd2 Bf5 6. Ngf3 Qb6|caption=The '''...c5''' approach. Steinitz countergambit mainline, after 2...c5 3. e3 Nc6 4. c3 Nf6 5. Nd2 Bf5 6. Ngf3 Qb6.}} {{Chess/board|moves=1. d4 d5 2. Bf4 Nf6 3. e3 g6 4. Nf3 Bg7 5. h3 O-O 6. Be2 c5 7. c3 Nc6|caption=The '''...g6''', King's Indian-style approach, after 2...Nf6 3. e3 g6 4. Nf3 Bg7 5. h3 O-O 6. Be2 c5 7. c3 Nc6.}}
== History == Possibly the earliest example of 1. d4 d5 2. Bf4 was given by [[wikipedia:Jacob Sarratt|Jacob Sarratt]] in his 1808 book ''A Treatise on the Game of Chess''. This continued 2...Bf5 3. Nc3 Nc6 4. f3.{{Cite book |title=A Treatise on the Game of Chess |last=Sarrat |first=J. H. |publisher=William Miller |year=1808 |location=London |pages=92-94 |url=https://archive.org/details/bub_gb__B4CAAAAYAAJ |chapter=Twelfth Game}} James Mason pioneered the London system continuation (i.e. where White plays e3, places a knight on d2 and their light square bishop on d3 or e2) in the 1882 Vienna tournament.[https://www.chessgames.com/perl/chess.pl?tid=80456&pid=10426 Vienna 1882 Game of James Mason - Chessgames.com] It became known as the London after the 1922 London tournament, where it was played by [[wikipedia:Jose Capablanca|Capablanca]][https://www.chessgames.com/perl/chessgame?gid=1093057 Capablanca v Reti, 1922 - Chessgames.com] and others. The London system is popular with amateurs. It is often recommended to beginners as White can develop in the same way irrespective of how Black chooses to respond. It is occasionally seen at the highest levels of chess, e.g. in Ding v Nepomniachtchi in their 2023 World Championship match.[https://www.chessgames.com/perl/chessgame?gid=2477747 Ding v Nepomniachtchi, 2023 - Chessgames.com] A recent innovation is to combine Bf4 and Nc3, the Rapport-Jobava London, developed by [[wikipedia:Richárd Rapport|Richárd Rapport]] and [[wikipedia:Baduur Jobava|Baduur Jobava]]. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}}.
